# Air Quality Management 2.0: Introducing a model for collaborative partnerships that effectively fight air pollution

TL;DR â Air Quality Management 2.0 is a concept that focuses on engaging key stakeholders in collaborative partnerships in order to create more effective, sustainable air quality management programs. Though air quality has improved substantially over the last 50 years, traditional air quality monitoring and management still faces a slew of challenges â such as poor data transparency and access, inadequate data analysis capacities, a lack of planning to ensure long-term sustainability, insufficient collaboration across relevant sectors, and a need for greater community engagement. By implementing Air Quality Management 2.0, stakeholders â including communities, regulators, analysts, and technologies â can engage in sustainable, effective action for cleaner air.

This blog is part of a series focusing on the concept of Air Quality Management 2.0, which aims to link together key stakeholders, from communities to industries, in the work to create sustainable air quality management programs and improve public health when it comes to the negative impacts of air pollution. [Read our white paper on Air Quality Management 2.0 here](https://www.clarity.io/breathe-london).

### **Where weâre at with air quality management**

Although air quality has improved substantially over the last half-century, there are still many gaps left behind in air quality monitoring and management â like the collection of more local-level data, especially in communities that suffer disproportionately from poor air quality â that result in serious public health consequences.

Bringing together a myriad of stakeholders â including communities, regulators, emissions reduction partners, innovative technology, data analysts, and industry â is an essential part of comprehensively addressing air quality management.

The exciting work of our partnership with [Breathe London](https://www.breathelondon.org/) â a project that brings together hybrid networks of regulatory-grade monitors and low-cost sensors to facilitate collaboration across stakeholders â acts as a blueprint that other cities around the globe can follow to improve their air.

The Breathe London network is composed of over 400 low-cost sensors across 33 different boroughs of the city, bringing access to air quality data to over 8 million citizens. Breathe London works from the ground up, supporting community-led monitoring projects and deploying low-cost sensors in ways that best reflect their needs.

[Read our customer story with Breathe London here to learn more](https://www.clarity.io/customer-stories/breathe-london).

### **The promise of collaborative air quality management**

Effective air quality management involves engaging a diverse range of stakeholders to drive positive policy change and action for cleaner air.

The introduction of new air quality monitoring technologies â such low-cost sensors which can be seamlessly integrated with regulatory monitoring â are enabling a wide range of applications with air quality data, including:

While implementing high-resolution air quality monitoring to collect high-quality data is a vital first step in air quality management, data alone does not always translate into tangible results. Meaningful engagement between stakeholders and impact-oriented data analysis are the keys that transform data into meaningful action.

### **Challenges that cities typically face when implementing air quality management at scale**

City agencies may come across a variety of challenges and pitfalls when beginning to implement air quality monitoring and management programs.

#### **Gaps in data**

When only regulatory-grade air quality monitors are used â which are more expensive and, thus, often few and far between when compared to low-cost sensors â it is easy to miss the complete variability in pollutant levels within a single urban area.Â

Monitoring networks can leave out hotspots and other areas of high air pollution in cities â especially those that have long suffered from disproportionately poor air quality levels.

Another pitfall is that this monitoring is often limited to [criteria air pollutants](https://ww2.arb.ca.gov/our-work/programs/criteria-air-pollutants#:~:text=Criteria%20air%20pollutants%20are%20air,5.) using traditional methods of measurement. However, data shows that health disparities are often driven by [hazardous air pollutants](https://www.epa.gov/haps) and [air toxics](https://www.epa.gov/urban-air-toxics/about-urban-air-toxics).

#### **Data analysis and access**

These traditional management programs can also have poor data transparency and offer inadequate public access to data. Whether due to data not being shared with the public or because it is not presented in a user-friendly way, inaccessible data means the public is missing out on crucial information about the air they are breathing.

These networks may have inadequate data analysis capacities when it comes to drawing key insights about air quality data that has been collected, especially when it comes to non-regulatory data use.

Programs can also lack evidence-based design and evaluation when it comes to emission reduction programs and policies, which interferes with the clear measurement of progress over time.

#### **Lack of stakeholder engagement and cross-sector collaboration**

Having air quality monitoring and management that does not operate in close contact with policy, stakeholder engagement, and other real-world data applications is another limitation that prevents an air quality management program from being effective.

Air quality management programs can sometimes lack in properly involving other sectors that are linked to air quality action â including transport, urban planning, and health agencies â in addition to environmental agencies in air quality management initiatives. These other sectors tend to only have limited involvement in air quality management work, despite having a crucial role in this multi-sectoral approach to improving the state of the air.

Cities may implement projects without plans to ensure the long-term sustainability of their infrastructure and operation, which prevents large-scale and long-lasting changes to the quality of their air.

#### **Community involvement**

By not adequately making space for community voices to be heard in air quality management decision-making, despite these communities disproportionately bearing the brunt of air pollution impacts, air quality management programs can fail to address actual community concerns.

It is also essential that they not only involve communities in what has happened in the past but also what is currently happening in regard to their air quality. Communities must be involved in present, active work to reduce air pollution and understand exposureâs impacts.

### **Introducing Air Quality Management 2.0**

The concept of Air Quality Management 2.0 emphasizes the importance of coordinating action across stakeholder groups, including communities, regulators, analysts, and technologies.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64ef88642e2c463dcadb2e75_VootOUayI0oWUCASNggiGB8jl-hcL8tlv1S81o87hL4i6eEZWI3pM2xrkEAwKQ3UBIuUNiQ-vkSB-Ow0CgZ9PcnU88VoKr_6FYPNiJMIhPb6dxulRyBVY7tBTURoWCyUW1r8LKb55tpXXru6NCf91cg.png)

#### **Communities**

For communities, Air Quality Management 2.0 means that:

Because recognizing community impacts is a key part of taking stock of air pollutionâs impacts on a city â and community engagement is essential to effectively addressing issues and bringing about change â the role of communities in air quality management cannot be understated.

#### **Regulators**

Regulators have the legal and professional responsibility for air quality management, making them a key actor to engage in air quality management work. Air quality regulations set the basis upon which clean air work can be expanded, enforced, and incorporated into more permanent policy frameworks.

Importantly, expanding the work to enforce air quality standards and providing strategies to achieve these targets requires the support of the public and of different stakeholders across sectors.

#### **Analysts**

The data analysis capacities that are necessary to drive policy changes and tangible improvement from air quality data are a huge undertaking â and one that regulatory agencies were not originally designed or resourced to tackle.

Sharing high-quality, calibrated air quality data across stakeholder groups helps to drive greater analyses and collaboration that are needed to tackle an issue as large as air pollution.

#### **Technologies**

Air quality monitoring technologies, like calibrated low-cost sensor networks, should be leveraged alongside traditional regulatory-grade monitoring systems to supplement their data and fill in the gaps left behind.

Low-cost sensor networks must be carefully designed and deployed â including the use of rigorous quality assurance/quality control procedures âÂ  to close these gaps and to provide high-quality data to support emissions reduction policies and programs.

### **Bringing Air Quality Management 2.0 to your city**

As more and more people recognize the dire impacts of air pollution and the need for a new approach to addressing these disparities in urban areas, Air Quality Management 2.0 is being embraced by many cities around the world.

[Breathe Cities](https://www.cleanairfund.org/news-item/breathe-cities-new-initiative-to-tackle-air-pollution-in-cities-around-the-world/), an initiative modeled on the work of Breathe London, will work to support effective air quality management in cities across the globe, especially prioritizing the reduction of air pollutionâs impacts on public health and the climate.

This partnership between [Bloomberg Philanthropies](https://www.bloomberg.org/), [C40 Cities](https://www.c40.org/), and the [Clean Air Fund](https://www.cleanairfund.org/) will provide funding to cities to support their air quality monitoring, community engagement, and capacity building as local leaders drive more equitable policy change.

At Clarity, we truly believe that the future of clean air is collaborative. We applaud the work of Breathe London and eagerly look forward to the positive impacts that the Breathe Cities initiative will make in cities across the globe.

Learn more about the [Breathe London model and download the Air Quality Management 2.0 white paper here](https://www.clarity.io/breathe-london).

Interested in measuring air quality for cleaner air and a healthier climate? [Get in touch with our team](https://click.clarity.io/learn-more?_ga=2.194797421.1954439789.1693245575-1501669537.1609875750) to learn more about our Sensing-as-a-Service solution for governments, businesses, and community organizations, using our [Clarity Node-S monitors](https://www.clarity.io/products/clarity-node-s) and [Modules](https://www.clarity.io/products/clarity-node-s) that do not depend on infrastructure like WiFi or power â making them especially resilient during environmental disasters.

# Collaborative Efforts in Air Quality Monitoring: A Global Perspective

Discover how global collaboration is revolutionizing air quality monitoring and uniting technology, data, and communities in the fight for cleaner air.

## **The hidden global battle for clean air**

Air pollutants affect everyone, everywhere. From bustling city streets to remote countryside areas, these unseen enemies â ranging from particulate matter to noxious gases â have a profound impact on our health, environment, and economies. Yet, this isn't just a local issue; it's a global crisis demanding a united front.

The fight for cleaner air is on, and it's happening on a global scale. Governments, businesses, and communities worldwide are coming together in a collaborative effort to turn the tide against air pollution. This article takes you inside the unseen battle for better air quality globaly, highlighting the critical role of air quality monitoring in our collective quest for a healthier planet.

## **The vanguard of air quality monitoring: technologies and innovations**

In the quest for cleaner air, technology plays a pivotal role. Advanced air quality monitoring technologies have emerged, offering new insights and fostering global collaboration. These technologies are at the forefront of understanding and combating air pollution.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/65c055993684ac48c3b48ebf_xThhQw1Y4byFU55NAzqMxTURBdEDSl02NfFMUZD_3E_xlbDL4LKRMXFPZ0DfStQJtdjOYIAmg0AZxafB411AedGiJjC9oaiOEKTvHlcg_0XRk5rBzChHFprtSpiA_CSHikuutt5cxUAN83wvglqV5ws.jpeg)

These technologies are not standalone solutions but part of a larger, collaborative effort. Countries and organizations around the world are pooling their data and insights, creating a comprehensive global air quality database. This collective approach enhances our understanding of pollution patterns, enabling targeted actions to improve air quality. It's a testament to how innovation, when harnessed together, can lead the charge in the global fight against air pollution.

## **Data-driven decisions: The role of analytics**

Data analysis in air quality monitoring is a game changer. It turns a bunch of numbers into insights we can actually use. This is where the magic happens: taking raw data from monitoring devices, calibrating it for accuracy, and making sense of it to help cities, businesses, and communities make smarter choices about air quality.

Clarity's [data analysis services](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-solution/project-support#Add-on-Services) take this to the next level. They dive deep into the data, giving clients not just information, but insights. This helps in making informed decisions, backed by science and stats.Â

For a deeper understanding of how data analysis is transforming air quality management, consider the insights from [Air Quality Management 2.0: Collaborating with researchers to drive deeper air quality analysis](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-management-2-0-how-researchers-drive-actionable-air-quality-intelligence-with-open-data-and-analysis), which emphasizes the importance of data analysis in driving actionable air quality insights.

The move towards data-centric approaches isn't just happening in one city or country; it's a global shift. Cities around the world are seeing the value of accurate, high-resolution data. It's not just about measuring air quality anymore. It's about understanding it, managing it, and ultimately, improving it for everyone.Â

This is how we fight air pollution, armed with data and ready to make a difference. To learn more about how cities are utilizing high-resolution air quality data for climate planning, read [why cities need higher resolution air quality data to plan for climate change](https://www.clarity.io/blog/why-cities-need-higher-resolution-air-quality-data-to-plan-for-climate-change).

## **Community and collaboration: grassroots to global**

Community involvement and international collaboration are key drivers in the fight against air pollution. Around the world, innovative partnerships between governments, NGOs, educational institutions, and citizens are setting new standards for what can be achieved through collective effort. These collaborations are not just about sharing data or technology; they're about uniting diverse groups with a common goal: clean air for all.

The success of these partnerships demonstrates the power of collaboration in addressing environmental challenges. By bringing together diverse perspectives and expertise, these initiatives are making significant strides in improving air quality and public health. As the world continues to grapple with pollution, the role of community and collaboration has never been more important. Through united efforts, it's possible to create a healthier, more sustainable future for the planet.

## **Policy and progress: navigating air quality regulations**

Air quality monitoring and policy development go hand in hand. The data collected from monitoring plays a crucial role in shaping regulatory standards and crafting effective air pollution control strategies. It's this data that helps policymakers understand the severity of air pollution, identify its sources, and evaluate the effectiveness of current policies. This understanding is essential for developing regulations that are both ambitious and achievable, aimed at significantly reducing pollution levels.

Navigating the complexities of air quality regulations presents both challenges and opportunities. On one hand, the need for comprehensive data can strain monitoring resources, especially in areas with limited infrastructure. On the other hand, advancements in monitoring technology, such as [low-cost sensors and data analysis services](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-solution), offer new opportunities for gathering the detailed data needed for effective policy-making.Â

These advancements not only empower local and global efforts to monitor air quality but also enhance the ability to respond dynamically to the findings, adjusting policies and strategies in real time to achieve cleaner air. In this way, the interplay between monitoring and policy development is a dynamic process that continually evolves, driven by both technological innovation and regulatory ambition.Â

The [evolution of air quality policies and interventions](https://www.clarity.io/blog/what-makes-a-measurable-impact-on-air-pollution-how-low-cost-air-sensors-are-enabling-a-new-paradigm-of-air-quality-policies-and-interventions), from traditional regulatory approaches to more targeted efforts enabled by advanced technology, underscores the critical role of data in environmental protection and public health initiatives.

## **Sensing the future: predictions and innovations ahead**

Air quality monitoring is on the brink of transformative advancements. Emerging technologies promise to redefine how we understand, manage, and ultimately improve the air we breathe on a global scale. Here's a glimpse into what's on the horizon:

Clarity is committed to staying at the forefront of these innovations. By embracing and contributing to the development of [next-generation air quality solutions](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-solution), Clarity aims to enhance the effectiveness of global efforts to monitor and improve air quality.Â

The emphasis on collaboration remains crucial; sharing insights, technologies, and strategies across borders and across industry sectors can accelerate progress toward a future where clean air is accessible to all. This collective journey towards innovation and sustainability is not just about creating advanced tools but about forging a global community united in the quest for a healthier planet.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/665a4b2525ae15e2737c33db_GKOrRGjHb_HI7EqEAITG7XBTETLZDgDT3wcqOAyibrpTTE0_EVOYuNMT4nUd6Dga2nQtTQci8nJIOosDef9aS9OvCSAZCcqcqnforOhs22YgiEzXgLGNBS7vZ9JuUytD8tHPcn2FQ3g_nZFBX-GNeic.png)

## **The journey toward cleaner air**

The battle against air pollution is a collective one, demanding efforts from every corner of the globe. As we've seen, the [technologies and strategies for monitoring and improving air quality](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-solution) are evolving rapidly, offering new hope and pathways to cleaner air. From traditional monitoring stations to [innovative low-cost sensors](https://www.clarity.io/products/clarity-node-s) and satellite observations, the tools at our disposal are more powerful and accessible than ever before.Â

Yet, technology alone isn't the answer. The key to turning the tide against air pollution lies in collaborationâbetween governments, businesses, communities, and individuals.

Everyone has a role to play in this fight. Whether it's contributing data through citizen science projects, implementing policies based on accurate monitoring, or simply being more aware of the air quality in our environments, each action adds up. The journey towards cleaner air is a complex one, filled with challenges but also opportunities to make a real difference.

As we look to the future, it's clear that the path to breathing easier is paved with collective action and innovation. We invite you to explore the air quality solutions that fit your needs and join us in the mission for cleaner air. We can build a healthier, more sustainable future. Ready to take the next step? [Build your solution](https://www.clarity.io/build-your-solution) with Clarity today and be part of the global effort to improve air quality.

SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
![Home](/themes/custom/soga/logo.png)

# From the Experts: Leveraging Collaborations to Address the Air Pollution Problem in East Africa

![Image of city in Africa](/sites/default/files/styles/max_650x650/public/images/2023-10/africa2023-report-image.png?itok=OvfEwnQr)

East Africa has made significant improvements in understanding and addressing air pollution and related health effects. While no country has successfully eliminated air pollution, countries that have had demonstrable success in emissions reduction have traditionally relied on the enforcement of ambient air quality standards with routine air quality monitoring. Some of the unique challenges facing East Africa include limited availability of air quality data due to limited monitoring infrastructure, a lack of data translation for policymakers, and resource and capacity constraints to utilize existing health monitoring systems.

We recently [hosted a webinar series](#https://www.healtheffects.org/meeting/webinar-series-air-pollution-and-health-east-africa) that brought together a panel of researchers from across Africa with a common goal: identifying opportunities for expanding the reach and capability of East African air pollution programs. The conversations covered a wide range of topics, including a focus on international and multicity collaborations between researchers, nonprofits, community activists, and policymakers to address existing research and data gaps. Below, we highlight key messages from the first two webinars that focused on availability and usability of air quality and health data and strengthening capacity for locally led research programs.

**Data to action**  
Continuous air quality monitoring provides reliable data for research, public advocacy, and decision-making and. Across East Africa, data gaps have led to the creative development of fit-for-purpose tools, such as the rapid adoption of low-cost sensors for air pollutant measurements.   
Experts in these webinars focused on data availability and usability. To make progress on air pollution, there must be compelling and trusted evidence of the issue, demonstrable impact on citizens and vulnerable populations, and clear demands to fix the issues through collaborative engagement across interested parties. Urgent and clear demands for improvements are more likely to be met by policy formulation and implementation. From a scientific and policy context, this raises an important question: Can air quality research in Africa be needs-driven?

Research and data providers can play the role of honest brokers who understand the needs and processes of decision-makers and other relevant users. Throughout the webinar discussions, experts emphasized the importance of harmonization and quality assurance of air quality data to enable its usage for research. While challenges remain, there are also areas where progress has been made. The [AirQo project](#https://www.airqo.net) in East Africa not only provides validated data in a variety of formats to different audiences but also provides access to an open-source resource to [calibrate sensor data](https://www.airqo.net/products/calibrate). 

![webinar 1 speakers](/sites/default/files/styles/cke_media_resize_medium/public/images/2024-02/webinar-1-speakers.png?itok=Rm-jPgSa)

**Building a health case for action**  
HEI’s [interactive literature database](https://www.healtheffects.org/global/interactive-database/east-africa) for East Africa shows growing evidence of the health effects of air pollution in the region. However, the range of health studies needs to be expanded to include studies that assess the effects of long-term exposure to air pollution; these are typically associated with a larger health and economic burden.

Health and Demographic Surveillance Systems (HDSS) offer an opportunity to strategically pair existing air quality monitoring datasets with health data to answer locally relevant questions. Across Africa, there are multiple HDSS sites that routinely collect mortality, morbidity, socioeconomic, and migration data. Various health outcomes are also monitored, including child, maternal, and cardiovascular. For instance, according to the [Nairobi Urban Health and Demographic Surveillance System](https://aphrc.org/project/nairobi-urban-health-and-demographic-surveillance-system-nuhdss/), respiratory tract infections accounted for 35% of deaths in children under 5 years between 2003 and 2016.

Collaboration with networks currently collecting HDSS data can greatly expand the scope of health studies in the region and help address existing research gaps. Some projects, such as the [Eastern Africa GEOHealth Hub](https://geohealth-hub.org/), are working to combine continuous air quality monitoring data with health data from hospitals to understand associations between air pollution and health.

![chair and speaker](/sites/default/files/styles/cke_media_resize_medium/public/images/2024-02/webinar2-chairs-speakers.png?itok=1fhiQlCH)

**Strengthening capacity**  
Another priority identified during the webinars included the need for further investment to strengthen air quality management capacity with the improvement of air quality monitoring infrastructure. Ongoing examples indicate that existing systems of collaboration can be leveraged to promote and share research by and for East Africans.

Improvements in air quality can be realized when capacity strengthening programs focus on long-term skill building and local resource development. For example, the CLEAN-Air Africa consortium has established an [Air Pollution Centre of Excellence](https://cleanairafrica.com/kemri-university-of-liverpool-air-pollution-centre-of-excellence/) that provides mentorship and support to academic training, including PhD programs and postdoctoral research. Similarly, the Pan African Thoracic Society (PATS) [MECOR](https://panafricanthoracic.org/pats-mecor) program provides training on research methods to promote education and research on lung health across Africa. There are emerging opportunities to promote learning from experiences within and across the region. Projects such as the [Clean Air Catalyst](https://www.cleanaircatalyst.org/) in Nairobi are bringing together a broad range of organizations to support local policy action.

Building scientific evidence on the health effects of air pollution in East Africa will require local, national, and regional efforts and collaborations across sectors and disciplines. When air quality improves, so does population health. 

This is the first part of a two-part blog on key messages from the HEI East Africa Air Pollution and Health Webinar Series.

Materials from the webinar series, including slides and recordings, are available on the [HEI website](https://www.healtheffects.org/meeting/webinar-series-air-pollution-and-health-east-africa). We would like to thank our cohosts, the [Pan African Thoracic Society](#https://panafricanthoracic.org/) and the [Respiratory Society of Kenya](https://www.resok.org/), for providing input and shaping the agenda for these webinars. We would also like to thank AirQo for their support in dissemination of workshop materials.

--- SOURCE 5: Synergies of air pollution control policies: A review - ScienceDirect ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0301479725006310

SUMMARY:
This study systematically reviewed 773 articles from 1998 to 2023 and, for the first time, integrated four primary domains of air pollution control policies.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Fostering dialogue with policymakers is crucial for implementing effective air quality regulations and ensuring data-driven decisions that prioritize public health and environmental sustainability.

Collaborating with research institutions enhances the capacity for data analysis, enabling evidence-based strategies that address local air quality challenges through innovative monitoring technologies.

enriched_points.point_title: Community involvement in air quality management
enriched_points.expanded_content: Empowering communities to participate in air quality monitoring initiatives ensures their concerns are addressed, building trust and fostering local ownership of air quality improvement efforts.
